name: Red Sea Maritime Security 2023-2024
description: A strategic shipping and security notebook showing how a narrow chokepoint in the Red Sea linked regional conflict, naval patrols, and global trade disruption.

sides.name: Launch and coercion network
sides.description: The operational logic was to threaten shipping and impose uncertainty from Yemen's Red Sea littoral.

subUnits.name: Houthi coastal launch network
subUnits.description:
Missile, drone, and maritime seizure capability positioned to pressure ships transiting toward or through the strait.
- Role: Coastal interdiction and signaling network
- Geographic anchor: Anchored along the Yemeni Red Sea coast, especially around Hodeidah and approaches facing the southern Red Sea.
- Why it matters: It turned a regional conflict into a maritime insurance and route-planning problem for global trade.
- What to watch: Launch tempo, target selection, and whether pressure remains focused on high-profile shipping or spreads more broadly.

sides.name: Security response
sides.description: Naval patrols aimed to protect transit, intercept threats, and reassure commercial operators without fully eliminating risk.

subUnits.name: Operation Prosperity Guardian
subUnits.description:
U.S.-led maritime protection effort that concentrated on escort, air defense, and reassurance for commercial shipping.
- Role: Escort and interception mission
- Geographic anchor: Operated across the southern Red Sea and Gulf of Aden near the main shipping lane.
- Why it matters: Its presence helped keep some traffic moving and established a shared reference frame for risk reporting and response.
- What to watch: How much escort presence is enough to restore confidence rather than merely react to attacks.

subUnits.name: EUNAVFOR ASPIDES
subUnits.description:
European maritime mission focused on defensive escort and protection of freedom of navigation in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.
- Role: European protection mission
- Geographic anchor: Built its picture around the same chokepoint system but with a distinct European command framework and ship set.
- Why it matters: It demonstrated how a trade shock can rapidly produce separate but overlapping security architectures.
- What to watch: Patrol coverage, convoy timing, and the relationship between European, U.S., and regional naval presence.

sides.name: Commercial operators
sides.description: Shipping firms, insurers, and crews transformed isolated attacks into a durable logistics story once they began rerouting at scale.

subUnits.name: Commercial carriers
subUnits.description:
The operational choices of liner companies and bulk operators became the clearest indicator of whether the corridor still felt usable.
- Role: Traffic and routing baseline
- Geographic anchor: Concentrated in the southern Red Sea transit lane and the alternate Cape route once risk pricing shifted.
- Why it matters: Routing decisions are what turned a regional threat into a global trade disruption story.
- What to watch: Transit volumes, insurer terms, crew confidence, and how long diversions remain economically acceptable.

events.title: Galaxy Leader seizure crystallizes the threat
events.description: The seizure of the Galaxy Leader made the Red Sea crisis legible to a global audience. What had been a regional warning rapidly became a practical reminder that ships could be singled out and physically stopped.

events.title: Major carriers begin broad rerouting decisions
events.description: Once operators started shifting ships around the Cape, the crisis became a test of schedule resilience and inventory timing, not just of naval interception.

events.title: Operation Prosperity Guardian is announced
events.description: The U.S.-led mission signaled that commercial protection would be organized as a sustained multinational maritime task rather than as a series of disconnected tactical responses.

events.title: MV Rubymar is hit and later sinks
events.description: The attack on Rubymar underlined that the danger was not only reputational or temporary. A ship could be disabled, abandoned, and become both a safety and environmental concern.

events.title: EU launches ASPIDES
events.description: Europe's dedicated mission reinforced the idea that the chokepoint had become a standing freedom-of-navigation problem with separate command structures converging on the same waters.

events.title: Attack on True Confidence brings fatalities
events.description: The strike on True Confidence highlighted the direct human cost of continued transit under threat and reinforced the caution already shaping routing decisions.

meta.name: Bab el-Mandeb Strait
meta.description: The narrow southern gateway into the Red Sea and the natural reference point for almost every shipping security discussion in the theater.

meta.name: Hodeidah coast launch arc
meta.description: The coastal zone most often referenced when discussing missile, drone, and maritime pressure emerging from Yemen.

meta.name: Main Gulf of Aden transit lane
meta.description: Simplified line of the eastbound and westbound commercial route exiting the strait toward the Gulf of Aden.

meta.name: Djibouti logistics hub
meta.description: Regional port and basing hub that matters for maritime monitoring, replenishment, and mission staging.

meta.name: Cape of Good Hope diversion
meta.description: Illustrative rerouting line around southern Africa used by operators choosing predictability over Red Sea transit.
